content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

March 7, 1996

Greg Schmidt
Executive Officer
Senate Rules Committee
California Legislature
State Capitol, Fourth Floor
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-072

Dear Mr. Schmidt:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Will any of the entities described below have made gifts or
campaign contributions to various senators participating in two
trade summits?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The use of the meeting rooms and refreshments provided
by the meeting co-sponsors will be contributions to the four
senators sponsoring the first meeting. No other gifts or
contributions will result.

FACTS

Two legislative committees of the California State Senate are
planning public meetings on the issues of trade with the Americas
and trade with the Asian Pacific Rim. Both events warrant the
extensive involvement of representatives of foreign governments
and industry representatives. Enumerated below are the facts of
each public meeting:

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-072
Page 2


PUBLIC MEETING #1

     The following senators are members of the Subcommittee on the
Americas: Senators Caleron, Polanco, Russell, and Solis.  Other
Senators may, at the time, choose to attend the public meeting.
The Subcommittee is planning a summit on trade issues in the
Americas with a specific focus on actual and potential trade
barriers for a number of California industries.  The summit will
be held in conjunction with a governmental trade conference and
exhibition.  The summit will include opening and closing remarks
in a general open forum and several concurrent panels.  The
sponsor of the trade conference and exhibition will make meeting
rooms available to the subcommittee for the summit which will have
been reserved, paid for and used before and after the summit by
the trade conference sponsor.  The anticipated duration of the
summit is approximately four hours.  Refreshments will be served
before the opening remarks and available during the panel
discussions.  A non-Senate entity will be asked to underwrite the
cost of the refreshments and will either (1) make the payments
directly to the vendor or, (2) reimburse the trade conference
sponsor.

     Opening and closing remarks will be made by federal
representatives and plenipotentiaries may be available to address
the summit.  Panel participants will also include a wide variety
of representatives from federal, state and local government and
representatives and agents of industries outside of the United
States.  In addition to the above, panel participants will include
representatives from a very broad section of California industry.
It is your assumption that these individuals are participating on
behalf of their businesses and trade groups.

PUBLIC MEETING #2

     Senators Killea and Solis are members of the Senate
Subcommittee on Asia Trade and Commerce. Other Senators may, at
the time, choose to attend the public meeting.  The subcommittee
is planning a Pacific Rim Forum to focus on existing barriers to
increase trade and investment between California and the Pacific
Rim.  The forum will be organized to include two keynote speakers,
several concurrent panels and a general session.  Keynote
participants will include representatives from the federal
government.  Presumably these representatives will participate in
their official capacities and government funds will be expended to
transport them to and from the forum as well as any preparation in
advance of the public meeting.

     Panel participants will also include representatives from
federal, state and representatives from foreign governments.
These participants are acting in their official capacities and not
as unpaid volunteers on their own time.  In addition to the above,
panel participants will include representatives from a very broad
section of California industry and foreign business interests.  It

File No. A-96-072
Page 3


is your assumption that these individuals will be participating on
behalf of their businesses and trade groups. The venue for this
public meeting is facilities at a California higher education
campus. The facilities use fee is substantial and will be waived
by the campus because the event is a public meeting held by the
state government. Refreshments will be made available to the
attendees and the participants. The refreshments will be served
at an opening reception and a closing dinner. The staff working
on the public meeting itself will probably have a break room where
refreshments will be served. A number of non-Senate entities
would like to underwrite the cost of the refreshments. They will
make the payments directly to the vendor providing the
refreshments.

The committee will prepare material for distribution before
and during the public meeting. Unsolicited material sent to
members of the public will be prepared in accordance with the mass
mailing regulation. However, in that material and in the material
for distribution at the public meeting, the committee will include
the names and titles of the participants. No logos, trademarks,
etc., of any non-Senate entity will be included in the material.
Photographs (of keynote speakers and panel participants) will be
permitted in the material distributed at the public meeting.
Concurrent with the public meeting, in an adjacent facility, a
number of entities will offer the public meeting participants an
exposition of California products, services and government
agencies that deal with trade and commerce. Typically, these
entities will set up booths and demonstration projects for the
public meeting participants to tour and inspect at their leisure.
The exposition participants will arrange facility use directly
with the campus. No legislative funds will be expended for the
planning, organizing or delivery of the exposition. However, a
notice of the availability of the exposition will be included in
the materials distributed before and during the public meeting.

APPLICABLE LAW

Section 82015 defines "contribution" as any payment,
forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that
full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes. A payment is made for political purposes if
it is: (1) for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or

File No. A-96-072
Page 4


passage of any measure; or (2) received by or made at the behest
of a candidate.2 (Regulation 18215(a).)

Section 82007 defines "candidate," in pertinent part, as any
individual who is listed on the ballot for elective office. An
individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her status
as a candidate until such time as that status is terminated
pursuant to Section 82214. Thus, as an elected official, the
State Senators are considered candidates under Section 82007.
Consequently, any payments a State Senator bestows are presumed
to be for political purposes and will be considered contributions
absent any exception. (Danner Advice Letter, A-96-039.)3

Regulation 18215 provides that a contribution includes "[a]ny
goods or services received by or behested by a candidate or
committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market
value, unless the discount is given in the regular course of
business to members of the public." (Regulation 18215(b)(3).)

Regulation 18215(c) further provides, in pertinent part,
that contribution does not include:

* * *

(2) Volunteer personal services or payments
made by a person for his own travel expenses, if
such payments are made voluntarily without any
understanding or agreement that he or she will be
repaid.

(3) A payment made by an occupant of a home
or office for costs related to any meeting or

_____________________
2 Regulation 18215 provides: "A payment is made for political
purposes if it is: (1) for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure; or (2) received by or
made at the behest of the following or any agent thereof: (A) a
candidate; (B) a controlled committee; (C) an official committee
of a political party, including a state central committee, county
central committee, assembly district committee or any subcommittee
of such committee; or (D) an organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes, including, but not limited to, a
political action committee established by any membership
organization, labor union or corporation.

3 A candidate "bebestows" a contribution when the contribution is
made "under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or
suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of."
(Regulation 18225.7(a).)

File No. A-96-072
Page 5


fundraising event held in the occupant's home or
office, if the total cost of the meeting or
fundraising event is $500 or less, exclusive of the
fair rental value of the premises.

(4) A payment made at the behest of a
candidate, which is for a communication by the
candidate or any other person, that meets all of
the following:

(i) Does not contain express advocacy;

(ii) Does not make reference to the
candidate's candidacy for elective office, the
candidate's election campaign, or the candidate's
or his or her opponent's qualifications for office;
and

(iii) Does not solicit contributions to the
candidate or to third persons for use in support of
the candidate or in opposition to the candidate's
opponent.

Where a payment is not a contribution, it may in some
circumstances be considered a gift. "Gift" is defined in Section
82028 as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or
greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in
the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status."

The term "gift" does not include material which serves
primarily to convey information and which is provided for the
purpose of assisting the recipient in the performance of his or
her official duties. (Section 82028; Regulation 18942.1.) Of
course the determination of whether a payment is a gift or
contribution is a factual question which must be determined on a
case-by-case basis.

ANALYSIS

Public Meeting #1

According to the information you have provided, Senators
Calderon, Polanco, Russell, and Solis have organized a public
meeting concerning trade issues. Thus, any payments made at the
behest of these Senators will be considered contributions absent
an exception.4

4 Other Senators may attend the event, however, you have not
indicated that they will be co-sponsoring, behesting any payments
to support the event or doing anything other than attending.
Therefore, anything of value received by these Senators, such as

File No. A-96-072
Page 6


     The sponsor of the trade conference and exhibition plan to
preserve and pay for meeting rooms for the summit. You have not
provided detailed information about the sponsors or the location
for the event, however, it does not appear that the sponsors will
be making their own offices available for the summit.
Accordingly, the payments made to provide the meeting rooms would
be contributions to the Senators on a pro rata basis. Similarly,
the refreshments provided at the event and paid for by the event
sponsors will be considered contributions to the Senators on a pro
rata basis.5

     The meeting will have a variety of presentations made by
representatives of federal agencies, foreign diplomatic
representatives, and representatives of industries located both in
the United States and outside the United States.

     Pursuant to Regulation 18420(b), the payment by a state or
local government agency of the salary or expenses of its employees
or agents is an expenditure or contribution only if the salary or
expenses are for campaign activities and meet the requirements of
Regulation 18423. Presumably, these participants will be
providing the presentation in the course of their employment. The
activities described are not "campaign activities" within the
meaning of Regulation 18420. Therefore, no contribution would
result by the use of legislative, local or federal personnel to
conduct the meeting. The exclusion would include reimbursement of
their travel expenses, if any.

     The same analysis would apply to the presenters who are
private employees as that discussed above with respect to public
employees. Regulation 18423 provides an exclusion from the
definition of "contribution" where an individual is paid by his or
her employer and the individual spends less than 10% of his or her
compensated time in a calendar month rendering services for
political purposes. The exclusion would include reimbursement for
personal expenses, such as travel, paid for by an employer.
(Regulation 18423(d).) Based on your facts, the personal services
would not be rendered for political purposes, as defined in
Regulation 18423(b).6


___________________________
food and drink at the event, will be considered gifts absent an
exception. (Regulation 18941.1.)

5 Generally, the fair market value of goods and services which
are a non-monetary contribution is whatever it would cost the
candidate receiving the goods or services to purchase them on the
open market. (Raful Advice Letter, No. A-95-241.)

6 For purposes of Regulation 18423, personal services are
rendered for "political purposes" if they are carried on for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of

File No. A-96-072
Page 7


     Because these services are not contributions, we must address
whether the services would constitute gifts to the Senators
instead. (Section 82028.) According to your facts, the
presentions would be provided primarily to convey information
and to assist the Senators in the performance of their official
duties or of their elective office. Therefore, the presentations
would be considered "informational material" and not gifts to the
Senators. (Regulation 18942.1.) Since no gift to the Senators
would result, the travel to the presenters paid for by their
employers would, similarly, not constitute gifts.

Public Meeting #2

     The issues raised in the second meeting are similar to that
of the first meeting. You anticipate several presenters from
government and private industry. These presentations would be
analyzed as personal services as described above in Public Meeting
#1.

     The location of Public Meeting #2 would be a higher education
campus. The fees for the venue would be waived because the event
is a public meeting held by the state government. So long as the
fees are regularly waived for any state entity holding a meeting
at the location, without regard to the particular state agency or
official requesting the waiver, the exception for discounts made
available to the general public will apply. (Regulation
18215(b)(1).) In re Russel (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 191.) If the
discount is not generally made available to all state agencies
without regard to status, the exception will not apply. (Schmidt
Advice Letter, No. A-96-056.)

     Refreshments, including a closing dinner, will be served at
the meeting and made available to attendees, participants and
staff. The non-Senate entities will underwrite the cost of the
refreshments at the behest of the co-sponsoring Senators.
Accordingly, the refreshments will be contributions to each of the
Senators on a pro rata basis.

     The Senators plan to prepare materials for distribution
before and during the public meeting. Payments for these
materials will not be considered contributions if they meet the
requirements of Regulation 18215(c)(4). Regulation 18215(c)(4)
provides that communications are not contributions if they do not
contain express advocacy, do not make reference to the candidate's


the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or
more candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18423(b).)

File No. A-96-072
Page 8


candidacy for elective office or the candidate's opponent for
elective office, and do not solicit contributions.

     Finally, you asked if an exposition attendant to the public
meeting may result in a gift or campaign contribution to Senators
Killea and Solis. Your facts indicate that the Senators will not be
involved in the planning or execution of the exposition. There
are no facts indicating that the Senators behested the payments
used to hold the exposition. In addition, the Senators will not
receive any payment or direct benefit from the exposition.
Accordingly, the payments used to hold the exposition will not be
considered either gifts or contributions.

     Please keep in mind that the advice in this letter is limited
to your specific inquiries regarding the gift and contribution
provisions of the Act and does not address such issues as mass
mailings or rules regarding lobbyists or lobbyist employers. If
you have any additional questions, please feel free to contact us
at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Liane Randolph
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak

campaign contributions to various senators participating in two
trade summits?

CONCLUSION: Yes.
The use of the meeting rooms and refreshments provided
by the meeting co—sponsors will be contributions to the four
senators sponsoring the first meeting.
No other gifts or
contributions will result.
